questions.question: What goes wrong when a server exposes everything — including plain reads — as tools?
options: The server runs out of tool slots
Every data access becomes a model-driven call, content can't be subscribed to, and client UIs like resource pickers can't surface the data
Tools cannot return text content
Nothing — tools-only is the recommended design
questions.explanation: Wrapping reads in tools forces the model to decide on a call for every query that might need context, and hides the data from host affordances like Claude Desktop's attachment panel or Cursor's file picker. Reads belong in resources.

questions.question: What is the decision rule for tool vs resource vs prompt?
options: Tools for Python servers, resources for TypeScript, prompts for both
Search/transform actions are tools; data the user attaches as context is a resource; a reusable multi-step workflow is a prompt
Anything under 1KB is a resource; larger is a tool
Use whichever primitive the SDK example uses
questions.explanation: If the model benefits from calling it per-query, it's a tool. If the user benefits from attaching it to a conversation, it's a resource. If a whole templated workflow is the reusable unit, it's a prompt.

questions.question: How do resource subscriptions work end to end?
options: The client polls resources/read every second
Server declares resources.subscribe, client calls resources/subscribe {uri}, server emits notifications/resources/updated on change, client re-reads
The server pushes new content directly into the model's context
Subscriptions are managed by the LLM provider
questions.explanation: Capability-gated push: a file watcher on the server triggers the updated notification, and the host re-pulls — e.g. Claude Desktop refreshing a file edited outside the app. Without subscribe capability, the client must not try.

questions.question: What does prompts/get return, and how do hosts surface prompts?
options: A single string; hosts paste it into the chat box
A filled message list ({role, content} sequence) from the template; hosts expose prompts as slash-commands with argument forms
A URL the user must open
A binary blob the model decodes
questions.explanation: A prompt is a template that expands into messages the host feeds its model — e.g. /code_review filling a system + user + assistant-kickoff sequence. Clients without prompt support simply never show the slash-commands.

questions.question: When must a dynamic resource URI include a timestamp or nonce?
options: Always — URIs must be unique per read
When the computation is one-shot, so the client's URI-keyed cache doesn't serve stale content; stable URIs are for cacheable content
Only for binary resources
Never — URIs are required to be permanent
questions.explanation: Resources can be computed (notes://recent, db://query/...). The caching contract is the rule: cache-by-URI requires a stable URI; one-shot computations need a nonce so caches don't stale out.
